steps.title: Analyze the structure
steps.content: Both halves of the sentence are independent clauses. The second clause explains the first by elaborating on how light intensity affects photosynthesis.

steps.title: Apply the punctuation rule
steps.content:
The blank ends "the chemical reaction rate of photosynthesis[blank]"; what follows is an independent clause ("as light intensity increases, so does the rate...") that explains the claim. A colon after an independent clause correctly introduces an explanatory clause.

A — "photosynthesis:": The colon ends the first independent clause and signals that the following clause explains it. Correct.

B — "photosynthesis,": A comma alone between two independent clauses is a comma splice. Fails.

C — "photosynthesis": No punctuation between two independent clauses creates a run-on sentence. Fails.

D — "photosynthesis and": "And" is a coordinating conjunction, but it requires a comma before it ("photosynthesis, and") and implies a parallel addition rather than an explanation. Without the comma it is still a run-on, and the logical relationship is wrong — the second clause explains, not merely adds. Fails.
